skill.name: vitest-testing
skill.description: Practical Vitest testing guidance with patterns for unit, integration, component, API, async, and error tests.
skill.summary: Use Vitest patterns to write clearer, behavior-focused tests for TypeScript and JavaScript projects.
skill.author: AdamManuel-dev

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command, network, filesystem, and credential patterns, but review shows most are markdown tutorial examples or code fences rather than marketplace-executed behavior. No prompt injection attempt or malicious exfiltration intent was found. The main confirmed concern is an unsafe password-hashing sample in a bundled TypeScript example, so publication is reasonable with a security warning.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe Password Hashing Example
medium_findings.description: The bundled UserService example stores passwordHash using a simple string prefix. A comment says to use bcrypt in reality, but users or agents could copy the insecure pattern into application code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The insecure hashing code is directly present in a TypeScript example and is semantically confirmed by the implementation. The risk is instructional copy-paste risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Examples Include Environment Mutation Patterns
medium_findings.description: Some tutorial examples read or write process.env values to model isolated test environments. This is legitimate testing guidance, but generated tests should restore environment state and avoid exposing real secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment access is visible in documentation examples and appears testing-related. It is a moderate operational risk if copied without cleanup or used with real secret values.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command findings are triple-backtick TypeScript examples in markdown. These are documentation delimiters, not Ruby backtick execution or shell invocation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines are markdown code fences around tutorial snippets. This strongly supports a false-positive conclusion for the bulk external_commands detections.

low_findings.title: Local Browser Storage Examples Are Test Fixtures
low_findings.description: Browser storage access appears in component testing examples that demonstrate localStorage behavior. It is not evidence of secret collection or persistent marketplace behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The storage calls are inside a documented Vitest hook test. The context is legitimate, but users should avoid storing sensitive data in browser storage.

low_findings.title: Network URLs Are Documentation and Local Test Targets
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to public documentation, localhost, or mock testing resources. No evidence found that the skill sends data to an external service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs are base URLs for local tests or documentation links. I did not find evidence of credential exfiltration or hidden network behavior.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Used for Vitest Mocking Guidance
low_findings.description: Dynamic import() appears in a Jest-to-Vitest migration example for partial module mocking. It does not load remote code or construct import paths from user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The import target is a local relative module inside a tutorial snippet. This makes arbitrary code loading unlikely in the reviewed context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Password Hash Placeholder
dangerous_patterns.description: A sample service returns a password hash by prefixing the plaintext password. Treat this as illustrative only and replace it with a real password hashing library in generated code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly returns a deterministic string derived from the plaintext password. The nearby comment confirms it is simplified rather than production-ready.

dangerous_patterns.title: Test Examples Touch Environment and Browser State
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples demonstrate process.env and localStorage usage. These are normal testing topics, but agents should isolate and restore state when applying the guidance.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The patterns are documented test examples with legitimate purpose. The residual risk comes from accidental state leakage in generated tests.

content.value_statement: Teams often write brittle tests that check implementation details instead of behavior. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Vitest patterns for clearer, isolated, maintainable tests.
actual_capabilities: Selects suitable Vitest test types using decision-tree guidance.
Applies F.I.R.S.T principles and Arrange-Act-Assert structure to test plans.
Provides patterns for mocks, stubs, spies, fakes, and dependency isolation.
Guides async, error, API, component, integration, and performance test design.
Helps migrate Jest examples and concepts to Vitest syntax.
Suggests refactoring patterns that make hard-to-test code easier to verify.
limitations: It is documentation guidance, not an automated test runner.
Some referenced README sections point to planned files that are not included in this package.
Security-sensitive examples need review before production use.
Generated tests still depend on the project structure and available dependencies.

use_cases.title: Add Feature Tests
use_cases.description: Create focused Vitest unit or integration tests for a new TypeScript feature using behavior-first assertions.

use_cases.title: Review Test Quality
use_cases.description: Check whether submitted tests follow isolation, clear setup, public API coverage, and meaningful assertions.

use_cases.title: Migrate From Jest
use_cases.description: Translate Jest mocks, matchers, and setup patterns into Vitest equivalents with fewer migration mistakes.

prompt_templates.title: Write a Basic Unit Test
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a small function or class method.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the vitest-testing skill to write a simple Vitest unit test for this function. Follow Arrange-Act-Assert and test public behavior only.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Existing Tests
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate cleanup for brittle or unclear tests.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Vitest tests with the vitest-testing skill. Identify weak assertions, shared state, implementation-detail checks, and missing edge cases.

prompt_templates.title: Design Integration Coverage
prompt_templates.scenario: Planning tests across services, APIs, or persistence boundaries.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the vitest-testing skill to propose an integration test strategy for this feature. Include dependencies to mock, data setup, and failure scenarios.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor for Testability
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for code that is difficult to test.
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the vitest-testing testability patterns to this code. Propose refactors, then outline Vitest tests that verify behavior without private method assertions.

output_examples.input: A developer asks for tests for a user registration service.
output_examples.output: The skill produces a behavior-focused test plan covering successful registration, duplicate email errors, validation errors, and email-service failure handling.

output_examples.input: A reviewer asks why a test is flaky.
output_examples.output: The skill highlights shared state, missing cleanup, time-dependent assertions, and recommends isolated setup with deterministic mocks.

output_examples.input: A team asks how to migrate a Jest mock to Vitest.
output_examples.output: The skill explains the Vitest mock pattern, shows which concepts map directly, and flags setup differences to verify.

best_practices: Start from observable behavior and public contracts before choosing mocks.
Keep each test isolated with fresh setup and explicit cleanup.
Use clear Arrange-Act-Assert sections so failures are easy to diagnose.
anti_patterns: Do not test private implementation details unless no public contract exists.
Do not share mutable test state across cases without reset logic.
Do not copy simplified security examples into production code without hardening.

faq.question: Does this skill run Vitest tests?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, patterns, and review criteria for writing Vitest tests.

faq.question: Can it help with React or Vue components?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes component testing guidance for hooks, state, events, and browser-like behavior.

faq.question: Does it support Jest migration?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a Jest-to-Vitest quick reference for mocks, imports, and setup differences.

faq.question: Is the included password hashing example production ready?
faq.answer: No. It is a simplified example and should be replaced with a real password hashing library.

faq.question: Can Claude Code use this for code review?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill provides checklist-style guidance for test quality, isolation, and black-box behavior.

faq.question: What projects benefit most from this skill?
faq.answer: TypeScript and JavaScript projects that use Vitest or are migrating from Jest benefit most.
